objective: Analyze live network traffic and a malware packet capture using Wireshark to identify protocol behavior, compare plaintext vs encrypted traffic, and document indicators of compromise (IOCs).
tools: Wireshark, Kali Linux, nslookup, ping, curl, Malware-Traffic-Analysis.net PCAP sample
steps:
1. Opened Wireshark on Kali and captured traffic on the active network interface.
2. Generated DNS traffic with nslookup and filtered on dns to identify query and response behavior.
3. Generated ARP traffic by pinging a local IP and filtered on arp to observe request/reply activity.
4. Tested HTTP traffic with curl http://example.com and confirmed the content was visible in plaintext.
5. Tested HTTPS traffic with curl https://example.com and filtered on tls to confirm the traffic was encrypted.
6. Downloaded a malware traffic exercise PCAP from Malware-Traffic-Analysis.net.
7. Extracted the password-protected PCAP from the terminal and opened it in Wireshark.
8. Reviewed Protocol Hierarchy and Conversations to identify the most active systems and protocols.
9. Filtered on dns, http.request, tls, smb, and smb2 to investigate host activity and external communications.
10. Pivoted on host 10.2.28.88 as the primary system of interest and documented IOC-style findings.
findings: Captured and analyzed working DNS query/response traffic. Captured ARP traffic and confirmed IP-to-MAC resolution on the local network. Confirmed HTTP traffic was readable in plaintext. Confirmed HTTPS traffic appeared as TLS-encrypted traffic. Identified 10.2.28.88 as the main host of interest in the malware PCAP. Observed internal DNS, CLDAP, SMB, and SMB2 traffic between 10.2.28.88 and 10.2.28.2. Documented outbound HTTP traffic from 10.2.28.88 to 23.47.50.182 (GET /connecttest.txt, Windows NCSI). Documented outbound TLS traffic between 10.2.28.88 and 13.89.179.9 (confirmed AS8075 Microsoft Corporation). All external connections ruled out as benign Windows/Microsoft infrastructure.
learned: Not every packet in a suspicious capture is actually malicious. Good analysis means identifying normal background traffic first, then isolating what deserves more attention. ARP happens before local communication even if the ping itself fails. HTTP vs HTTPS is much easier to understand when you see plaintext traffic next to TLS-encrypted traffic in Wireshark. Pivoting on one host made the malware PCAP much easier to work through.
tags: Wireshark, Packet Analysis, Network Forensics, DNS, ARP, HTTP, HTTPS, TLS, SMB, SMB2, IOC Analysis, Threat Hunting, Kali Linux, Blue Team, Security Operations, Security+
